title: YouTube
subtitle: The world's largest video platform, 2.7 billion monthly users, $34B in annual ad revenue, and the center of the global creator economy
content_html:
Overview

YouTube is the world's largest video-sharing platform, owned by Google LLC, a subsidiary of Alphabet Inc. As of 2026, YouTube hosts over 800 million videos and serves more than 2.7 billion logged-in monthly users, with total watch time exceeding 1 billion hours per day. The platform operates in over 100 countries and supports content in 80 languages.

YouTube functions simultaneously as a social media network, a search engine, a subscription streaming service, a live broadcasting platform, and the primary infrastructure layer of the global creator economy. It is the second most visited website in the world after Google Search, and for users under 35, it is the most-used source of news, entertainment, education, and long-form video globally.

Google acquired YouTube in October 2006 for $1.65 billion in stock, in what was then considered an audacious overpayment. By 2026, YouTube's advertising revenue alone exceeds $34 billion annually, making it one of the most valuable single digital media properties in existence. The acquisition is widely cited as one of the most successful technology deals of the 21st century.

History and Founding

YouTube was founded in February 2005 by three former PayPal employees, Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim, operating out of a garage in San Mateo, California. The founding insight was simple: there was no easy way to share video on the internet in 2004. The platform's first video, "Me at the Zoo," was uploaded by Karim on April 23, 2005, and remains live on the platform today.

YouTube launched publicly in November 2005 and grew explosively. By mid-2006, the site was hosting 65,000 new video uploads per day and serving 100 million video views daily. That scale, combined with the obvious advertising potential, prompted Google's acquisition in October 2006. The $1.65 billion price tag was paid in Alphabet stock, meaning the actual cost in 2026 dollars is substantially higher due to the stock's appreciation.

The platform's commercial structure evolved significantly over the first decade. YouTube launched the Partner Program in 2007, allowing creators to share in advertising revenue for the first time. The introduction of pre-roll advertising in 2008, HD video support in 2008, and live streaming in 2011 each marked structural expansions of the platform's commercial and technical scope. YouTube Premium, the paid subscription tier offering ad-free viewing and background playback, launched in 2018 as the platform's second major revenue stream.

The Creator Economy | YouTube's Defining Structural Role

YouTube is the foundational infrastructure of the modern creator economy. The YouTube Partner Program has paid out over $70 billion to creators, artists, and media companies in the last three years alone, according to Google's reported figures. More than 3 million channels participate in monetization globally, with the top tier of creators generating annual incomes equivalent to mid-sized media companies.

The platform's creator tier system ranges from hobby channels with a few thousand subscribers to institutional channels operated by major broadcast networks and studios. In between sits the segment that defines YouTube's cultural influence: independent creators with audiences of 100,000 to 10 million subscribers who produce content that competes directly with television, podcasting, and traditional media for audience time and advertising dollars.

Creator compensation flows through multiple mechanisms simultaneously: AdSense revenue sharing (the core Partner Program), channel memberships, Super Chat and Super Thanks (viewer tip mechanisms during live streams and on videos), merchandise shelf integration, brand deal disclosures managed through YouTube's native sponsorship tools, and direct YouTube Premium revenue allocated by watch time from premium subscribers. Creators with diversified revenue across all these mechanisms have proven significantly more resilient to algorithm changes than those dependent solely on AdSense.

The creator economy's center of gravity has shifted toward YouTube over TikTok and Instagram for long-form revenue, partly because YouTube's ad rates for 10-plus minute videos remain substantially higher than short-form competitor platforms, and partly because YouTube's search indexing means content continues earning for months or years after upload, while social media content typically earns within 48 hours of posting and then goes dormant.

YouTube Shorts | Short-Form Video Strategy

YouTube Shorts launched globally in July 2021 as YouTube's direct response to TikTok's explosive growth. Shorts allows creators to upload vertical videos up to 60 seconds (extended to 3 minutes in 2024) and surfaces them through a dedicated swipe-feed interface that mirrors TikTok's discovery mechanism. By 2026, Shorts generates over 70 billion views per day globally, making it one of the highest-volume short-form content ecosystems in existence.

Shorts monetization was added in February 2023 through a revenue-sharing model based on views and engagement. The revenue per thousand views (RPM) for Shorts remains lower than long-form YouTube content due to shorter ad insertion opportunities, but for creators whose content naturally formats as short-form, the incremental revenue adds meaningfully to total channel income.

YouTube's strategic advantage in Shorts over standalone short-form platforms is ecosystem integration. A creator who builds a Shorts audience can convert that audience to long-form subscribers, live stream viewers, and channel members. Standalone TikTok creators cannot convert their audiences to other products without directing them off-platform. This integration advantage has made Shorts a creator acquisition funnel rather than merely a competing format.

Monetization | Revenue Streams and Advertiser Economics

YouTube's revenue is dominated by advertising, which accounts for approximately 85% of total platform revenue. Google reports YouTube advertising revenue separately from other Google Services segments: it reached $34.7 billion in 2025, up from $31.5 billion in 2024. The remaining 15% comes from YouTube Premium subscriptions, YouTube TV (a live television streaming service operating in the United States), and channel membership and Super Chat revenue that flows through Google's payment infrastructure.

Advertising on YouTube operates through Google Ads and YouTube Select, the latter being the premium placement tier that puts brand advertising alongside the top 5% of content by viewership and brand safety score. CPM (cost per thousand impressions) varies significantly by content category: finance, technology, and business content consistently commands CPMs of $15 to $40, while gaming and entertainment content typically commands $4 to $12 CPM in North American markets. These rate differentials directly influence which content categories attract the most professional creator investment.

YouTube Premium had an estimated 100 million subscribers globally as of late 2025. Premium subscribers receive ad-free playback, background play (video continues playing when the app is minimized), offline downloads, and access to YouTube Music. A portion of Premium subscription revenue is distributed to creators based on their share of Premium subscriber watch time, typically adding 15 to 25% to a channel's total monthly earnings from YouTube.

Policy and Content Moderation

YouTube's content policies are enforced through a combination of automated machine learning systems, human review teams, and community flagging. The platform removes hundreds of millions of videos per quarter for policy violations, the large majority caught by automated systems before receiving meaningful views. Primary enforcement categories include child safety (CSAM and predatory content), violent extremism and terrorism, spam and scam networks, coordinated deceptive behavior, and medical misinformation under policies updated in 2022 and 2023.

The platform operates a three-strike system for channel-level enforcement. A first strike results in a temporary restriction on uploading and live streaming. A second strike within 90 days of the first doubles the restriction period. A third strike within 90 days of the second results in permanent channel termination. Major single violations, including CSAM, terrorism promotion, and certain coordinated manipulation behaviors, result in immediate termination without the strike process.

YouTube's relationship with news and political content has been one of the most contested aspects of its moderation posture. The platform's recommendation algorithms were documented in multiple third-party studies between 2018 and 2022 as amplifying increasingly extreme political content through recommendation chains. YouTube made multiple algorithm adjustments beginning in 2019, including reducing recommendations of "borderline content" and news from channels that had previously violated policies. The effectiveness and consistency of these adjustments remains an active area of academic and regulatory scrutiny.

In 2025, YouTube updated its synthetic media disclosure policy to require creators to label any content containing AI-generated or AI-altered audio or video. Content depicting realistic synthetic portrayals of real people must be labeled at upload, and YouTube reserves the right to add its own disclosure labels to content it detects as AI-generated when the creator has not self-disclosed. This policy aligns YouTube with the disclosure requirements that major social platforms implemented in the 2025 to 2026 period following federal and EU legislative pressure.

Competition | TikTok, Netflix, and the Attention Economy

YouTube faces competition across multiple product dimensions simultaneously. In short-form video, TikTok remains the primary rival, though YouTube Shorts' scale and ecosystem integration have eroded TikTok's differentiation advantage among creators who already have an established YouTube presence. Instagram Reels competes in the same short-form space but has not demonstrated the discovery algorithm quality that drives creator acquisition at scale.

In premium long-form and episodic content, YouTube competes with Netflix, Amazon Prime Video, and Disney+ for viewer attention time. YouTube's structural advantage is that it offers both professional studio content and creator content in the same interface, allowing it to capture viewing time across a wider range of user intent than any SVOD platform. Its disadvantage is that it does not produce exclusive tentpole content at the scale of Netflix Originals, which drives subscription acquisition.

Podcast and audio content has become an increasingly important competitive front since YouTube began prominently featuring podcast feeds and video podcasts in its interface in 2023. Spotify remains the dominant audio podcast platform by listener hours, but YouTube's video podcast initiative, which gives podcast audiences a passive viewing experience during recording sessions, has captured a significant share of the premium podcast audience that previously consumed audio only.

YouTube in 2026 | Platform Updates and Creator Economy Shifts

YouTube's primary platform focus in 2026 has been AI integration across both the creator toolkit and the viewer experience. The YouTube Dream Screen AI background generator for Shorts, launched in late 2023, was expanded in 2025 to support full background video generation. AI-assisted auto-dubbing, which generates voice-matched translated audio tracks for creator videos, was extended to 40 languages in early 2026, allowing channels to reach non-English-speaking audiences without manual translation work.

The AI content disclosure policy updates, described in the policy section, represent the most significant compliance-facing change of 2026. Creators producing synthetic media are now required to self-label, and YouTube has deployed detection systems that flag suspected AI-generated content for creator notification and, in cases of non-disclosure, for platform-applied labeling. This mirrors the disclosure regimes being deployed across major social platforms under pressure from the EU's AI Act and evolving U.S. FTC guidance.

Creator monetization thresholds were lowered in late 2023 and the changes have continued to compound into 2026. The YouTube Partner Program now admits channels with as few as 500 subscribers and 3,000 watch hours in the past 12 months for limited monetization features, down from the prior threshold of 1,000 subscribers and 4,000 hours. The lower threshold has expanded the monetized creator pool significantly while compressing per-channel average earnings, as advertising inventory is spread across a larger number of qualifying channels.

Follow all YouTube platform and creator economy updates at the OzoneNews Tech desk and coverage of individual YouTube creators at the OzoneNews Influencer hub.
